steps.title: Tie evidence to clam gardens
steps.content: The blank must follow from the archaeological evidence about the Kwakwaka'wakw terraced garden method. The songs describe the use of intertidal terraced gardens to cultivate clams for food, and the evidence shows significant increases in clam size and abundance during the same period when that method was implemented.

steps.title: Reject unsupported claims
steps.content:
• Choice B - 'non-Indigenous people around the Pacific Northwest coast adopted the practice developed by the Kwakwaka'wakw people after observing its efficacy.': The passage never mentions non-Indigenous adoption of the practice. Evidence that the method was effective does not show that other groups copied it.
  • Choice C - 'there is greater corroboration in the archaeological record of ecological practices described in Kwakwaka'wakw songs than of those described in Sakha and Kaluli songs.': The Sakha and Kaluli songs are only background examples. The passage provides no archaeological comparison involving them.

steps.title: Draw the limited conclusion
steps.content:
• Choice D - 'although contemporary Kwakwaka'wakw people have a deep understanding of and appreciation for the fishing and farming practices used by their ancestors, they no longer implement those methods.': The evidence concerns past clam cultivation, not current practices.
  • Choice A - 'the practice used by ancestors of modern Kwakwaka'wakw people not only effectively maintained a food source but also promoted its robustness.': Increased abundance supports maintaining the food source, and increased size supports promoting its robustness. The answer is A.
